subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 13! In this unit you will become an expert on measuring the world around you. Measuring means finding out how much of something there is – how heavy it is, how hot or cold it is, and how much a container can hold. You already measure things every day without even knowing it. When Amina lifts a bag of rice at the Hargeisa market and says it is too heavy to carry, when Yusuf feels that the morning air is cold before Fajr prayer, or when Halima pours milk into a jug for the family, they are all thinking about measurement.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Understand that mass means how heavy or light an object is, and compare objects to say which is heavier.
Use a balance scale to compare mass, and understand that the heavier side goes down.
Know that grams (g) and kilograms (kg) are units of mass, and choose the right one for an object.
Understand that temperature means how hot or cold something is, and read a simple thermometer.
Know that temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C), and understand what common temperatures feel like.
Understand that capacity means how much a container can hold, and compare containers by filling and pouring.
Know that millilitres (ml) and litres (l) are units of capacity, and choose the right one for a container.
Estimate mass, temperature and capacity, then measure to check, and solve real-world measuring problems.

concepts.title: Mass – Heavy and Light
concepts.explanation: Mass is a special word that means how heavy or light an object is. When you pick something up, your arms feel the mass. A big stone has a large mass, so it feels heavy. A feather has a tiny mass, so it feels light. We use the words heavy, light, heavier than and lighter than to compare the mass of things. Here is a very important idea, and it surprises many people: size does not always tell you the mass. Something big is not always heavier than something small. Think about a large balloon and a small stone. The…
concepts.example: Let us think of more examples from around the home. A large empty cardboard box feels light, but a small bag of coins feels heavy. A big soft cushion is light, but a small tin of tomatoes is heavier. Try this yourself:…

concepts.title: Comparing Mass with a Balance Scale
concepts.explanation: A balance scale is a tool that helps us compare the mass of two objects fairly. It has two pans, one on each side, joined in the middle. When you put an object in each pan, the pan holding the heavier object goes down, and the pan holding the lighter object goes up. It works just like a see-saw in a playground: the heavier child sinks down and lifts the lighter child up. Before you place objects on the scale, it is a good habit to predict which will be heavier. Look at the two objects, pick them up, and make your…
concepts.example: A balance scale is a tool that helps us compare the mass of two objects fairly. It has two pans, one on each side, joined in the middle. When you put an object in each pan, the pan holding the heavier object goes down,…

concepts.title: Measuring Mass in Grams and Kilograms
concepts.explanation: Comparing tells us which object is heavier, but sometimes we need to know exactly how heavy something is. To do this we use standard units that everyone in the world agrees on. The two units of mass you will learn are the gram and the kilogram. We write g for gram and kg for kilogram. A gram is very small and light. A single paperclip has a mass of about 1 gram. A small button, a single date, or a coin are all only a few grams. Grams are used for light things that you can lift easily with one hand.
concepts.example: The clever part is choosing the right unit. Ask yourself: is this object light enough to hold easily in one hand? If yes, measure it in grams. Is it heavy, needing two hands or a strong lift? If yes, measure it in…

concepts.title: Temperature – Hot and Cold
concepts.explanation: Temperature tells us how hot or how cold something is. You feel temperature all the time. The warm sun on your face during the day is a high temperature. A cup of cold water from the well is a low temperature. Warm camel milk, cool morning air, and hot bariis fresh from the pot all have different temperatures. For a long time people could only say “it feels hot” or “it feels cold”, but feelings are not exact. What feels hot to Zahra might feel just warm to Omar. To measure temperature exactly, we use a special…
concepts.example: Here is the golden rule for temperature numbers: a higher number means hotter, and a lower number means colder. So a temperature of 35 is hotter than a temperature of 20. And a temperature of 5 is colder than a…

concepts.title: Reading Thermometers and Degrees Celsius
concepts.explanation: We measure temperature in a unit called degrees Celsius, written as °C. So instead of just saying “it is warm today”, we can say “it is 30°C today”, which tells everyone exactly how warm. The little circle in °C is part of the sign, and we say it as “degrees”. 0°C is very cold – this is the temperature at which water freezes into ice.
concepts.example: 20°C is a comfortable room temperature – not too hot and not too cold.

concepts.title: Capacity – How Much Can It Hold?
concepts.explanation: Capacity means how much a container can hold. A container is anything that can be filled, such as a cup, a bottle, a bowl, a jug or a bucket. A container with a large capacity can hold a lot, like a big cooking pot. A container with a small capacity holds only a little, like a teaspoon. We use the words holds more, holds less, full and empty to talk about capacity. Just like with mass, there is a surprise to watch out for: the shape of a container does not always tell you its capacity. Many people think a tall…
concepts.example: How do we test which container holds more? We fill and pour. Fill the first container right to the top with water or with rice or sand. Then pour it carefully into the second container. If the second container…

methods.title: How to compare the mass of two objects
methods.example: Amina puts a mango in one pan of a balance scale and a lime in the other. The mango side goes down. Which fruit is heavier?
steps: Pick up one object in each hand, or place one in each pan of a balance scale.
The heavier object pulls its hand down, or its pan goes down.
If the scale stays level, the two objects have the same mass.
Say it in words: this object is heavier than / lighter than that one.

methods.title: How to choose the right unit
methods.example: Would you measure the mass of a single date in grams or in kilograms?
steps: For mass: light and easy to lift = grams; heavy = kilograms.
For capacity: a small amount = millilitres; a large amount = litres.
For temperature: always use degrees Celsius.
Estimate first with a benchmark, then measure to check.

methods.title: How to compare capacity
methods.example: One thermometer reads 15°C and another reads 32°C. Which is hotter, and by how much is the number bigger?
steps: Fill the first container to the top.
Pour it carefully into the second container.
Overflows = the first holds more. Space left = the second holds more.
Fills exactly = the two have the same capacity.

methods.title: Order temperatures
methods.example: Put these temperatures in order from coldest to hottest: 20°C, 0°C, 37°C, 10°C.
steps: Solution: Treat them like ordinary numbers and order from smallest to largest: 0, 10, 20, 37.
So coldest to hottest is 0°C, 10°C, 20°C, 37°C.
Remember, 0°C is freezing cold and 37°C is as warm as your body.

methods.title: Compare capacity by pouring
methods.example: Hassan fills a tall thin bottle with water and pours it into a short wide bowl. The bowl is not yet full – there is still space left. Which container has the greater capacity?
steps: Solution: All the water from the bottle fits into the bowl with space left over.
That means the bowl can hold more than the bottle.
So the bowl has the greater capacity, even though the bottle is taller.
This is the surprise: tall does not always mean more.

methods.title: Choose millilitres or litres
methods.example: Would you measure the water in a big bucket in millilitres or in litres?
steps: Solution: A big bucket holds a large amount of water.
Large amounts are measured in litres.
So we measure the bucket in litres.
(A teaspoon of medicine, which is a small amount, would be measured in millilitres.)

workedExamples.title: Compare two masses
workedExamples.prompt: Amina puts a mango in one pan of a balance scale and a lime in the other. The mango side goes down. Which fruit is heavier?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: On a balance scale, the side that goes down holds the heavier object. The mango side went down, so the mango is heavier than the lime. We could also write: mango is heavier than lime, lime is lighter than mango.

workedExamples.title: Choose grams or kilograms
workedExamples.prompt: Would you measure the mass of a single date in grams or in kilograms?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A single date is very light and easy to hold in one hand. Light objects are measured in grams. So we measure a date in grams. (A whole sack of dates, which is heavy, would be measured in kilograms.)

workedExamples.title: Read and compare temperatures
workedExamples.prompt: One thermometer reads 15°C and another reads 32°C. Which is hotter, and by how much is the number bigger?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A higher number means hotter. 32 is bigger than 15, so 32°C is hotter. The difference is 32 − 15 = 17, so 32°C is 17 degrees warmer than 15°C.

workedExamples.title: Order temperatures
workedExamples.prompt: Put these temperatures in order from coldest to hottest: 20°C, 0°C, 37°C, 10°C.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Treat them like ordinary numbers and order from smallest to largest: 0, 10, 20, 37. So coldest to hottest is 0°C, 10°C, 20°C, 37°C. Remember, 0°C is freezing cold and 37°C is as warm as your body.

workedExamples.title: Compare capacity by pouring
workedExamples.prompt: Hassan fills a tall thin bottle with water and pours it into a short wide bowl. The bowl is not yet full – there is still space left. Which container has the greater capacity?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: All the water from the bottle fits into the bowl with space left over. That means the bowl can hold more than the bottle. So the bowl has the greater capacity, even though the bottle is taller. This is the surprise: tall does not always mean more.

workedExamples.title: Choose millilitres or litres
workedExamples.prompt: Would you measure the water in a big bucket in millilitres or in litres?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: A big bucket holds a large amount of water. Large amounts are measured in litres. So we measure the bucket in litres. (A teaspoon of medicine, which is a small amount, would be measured in millilitres.)

workedExamples.title: A measuring word problem
workedExamples.prompt: A water bottle holds 500 millilitres. Khadija pours two full bottles into a jug. How many millilitres are in the jug, and is that more or less than 1 litre?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Two bottles of 500 ml each: 500 + 500 = 1000 millilitres. And 1000 millilitres is exactly 1 litre. So the jug holds 1 litre, which is the same as, not more than, 1 litre.

workedExamples.title: Estimate then check
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf holds a bag of flour and thinks it feels about the same as a bag of sugar, which he knows is 1 kilogram. He then weighs it and the scale shows 2 kilograms. Was his estimate close, and what should he learn?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: His estimate was 1 kilogram, but the real mass was 2 kilograms, so the bag was heavier than he thought. His estimate was in the right unit (kilograms) but too small. Next time he can remember that this size of bag feels like 2 kilograms. Estimating gets better every time you check it against a real measurement.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Put these temperatures in order from coldest to hottest: 25°C, 5°C, 37°C, 15°C.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A water bottle holds 500 ml. How many millilitres do two bottles hold together? Is that 1 litre?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A tall thin cup is poured into a short wide bowl and there is space left over. Which holds more?
workedExamples.solution: 500 + 500 = 1000 ml, and yes, that is exactly 1 litre.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: A jug holds 2 litres. How many 500 ml bottles are needed to fill it? Show your thinking.
workedExamples.solution: 1) 4 bottles – 500 ml + 500 ml = 1 litre, and 2 litres needs two lots, so 4 bottles.

activities.title: Heavy or Light? (Sorting Game)
activities.materials: several household objects (a spoon, a stone, a cushion, a book, an empty bottle, a full bottle).
steps: Hold two objects, one in each hand, and feel which is heavier.
Make three piles: Light, Medium and Heavy.
Find a big object that is light and a small object that is heavy – show them to your family.
Say out loud: This small one is heavier than this big one.
This game teaches you that size does not always tell you the mass.

activities.title: Make a Balance Scale
activities.materials: a coat hanger or a ruler, two cups or bags, and some string.
steps: Tie a cup to each end of the ruler or hanger so it hangs level.
Hang it from the middle so both sides balance.
Put an object in each cup and watch which side goes down – that side is heavier.
Try to find two objects that make the scale stay level – they have the same mass.

activities.title: Kitchen Capacity Pouring
activities.materials: water or rice, and different containers (a cup, a bottle, a bowl, a jug).
steps: Guess which container holds the most, and which holds the least.
Fill one container and pour it into another to test which holds more.
Put all your containers in order from holds least to holds most.
Were any surprises? Did a short wide bowl beat a tall thin bottle?

activities.title: Temperature Detective (Investigation)
activities.materials: cups of water at safe temperatures (cool, room temperature, warm – never hot).
steps: Feel each cup and guess which is warmest and which is coolest.
Put the cups in order from coolest to warmest by feel.
Talk about where you feel each temperature in real life – cool well water, warm camel milk.
Draw a simple thermometer and mark 0°C, 20°C and 40°C on it.

activities.title: Grams and Kilograms Hunt
activities.materials: objects from around your home and, if you have one, a kitchen scale.
steps: Find three objects you would measure in grams (light things).
Find three objects you would measure in kilograms (heavy things).
If you have a scale, weigh them and see if you were right.
Make a poster: Things in grams on one side, Things in kilograms on the other.

activities.title: Family Measuring Challenge (Game)
activities.materials: your family or friends, and a few containers and objects.
steps: One person picks an object or container and asks: Which unit would we use?
Everyone guesses grams, kilograms, millilitres, litres or degrees Celsius.
Test it if you can – lift it, pour it, or feel it.
Score a point for each correct unit choice. Play until someone reaches ten points.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand measuring best? What surprised you about heavy and light, or about which container held more? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Mass Rule
rules.text: Mass is how heavy or light an object is. On a balance scale, the heavier side goes down. Grams for light things, kilograms for heavy things. 1 kilogram = 1000 grams.

rules.title: Temperature Rule
rules.text: Temperature is how hot or cold something is, measured in degrees Celsius (°C). A higher number means hotter. Water freezes at 0°C; body temperature is about 37°C.

rules.title: Capacity Rule
rules.text: Capacity is how much a container can hold. Test it by filling and pouring. Millilitres for small amounts, litres for large amounts. 1 litre = 1000 millilitres.

terms: How heavy or light an object is
A tool that compares two masses; heavier side sinks
How hot or cold something is
How much a container can hold
commonMistakes: A big balloon is lighter than a small stone – lift to check
The side that goes down is the heavier one
A higher number means hotter: 30°C beats 10°C
A short wide bowl can beat a tall thin bottle – pour to check

realProblems.prompt: At the Eastleigh market, Sumaya buys 3 kilograms of mangoes and 2 kilograms of bananas. What is the total mass she carries home?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Liban fills the cooking pot with 4 jugs of water. Each jug holds 1 litre. How many litres are in the pot?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Early in the morning in Berbera the thermometer shows 19°C. In the afternoon it shows 41°C. How many degrees warmer is the afternoon?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Noor pours 3 cups of milk into a jug. Each cup holds 200 millilitres. How many millilitres are in the jug altogether?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: During Eid, Aisha weighs two baskets of dates. One is 6 kilograms and the other is 9 kilograms. Which basket is heavier, and by how much?
realProblems.answer: The 9 kg basket is heavier, by 9 − 6 = 3 kilograms.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The 9 kg basket is heavier, by 9 − 6 = 3 kilograms.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A jug holds 2 litres. How many 500 ml bottles are needed to fill it? Show your thinking.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Order these masses from lightest to heaviest: 15 kg, 3 kg, 20 kg, 8 kg.
Yusuf says a taller container always holds more water. Is this always, sometimes, or never true? Explain with an example.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Sometimes true – it works when a tall container is also wide, but a short wide bowl can beat a tall thin bottle; you must pour to be sure.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A bag of sugar is about 1 kilogram. About how many bags would balance a 4-kilogram sack of flour?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: About 4 bags of sugar, because each is about 1 kilogram and the sack is about 4 kilograms.

So instead of just saying “it is warm today”, we can say “it is 30°C today”, which tells everyone exactly how warm. The little circle in °C is part of the sign, and we say it as “degrees”. 0°C is very cold – this is the temperature at which water freezes into ice.

A tool that compares two masses; heavier side sinks

selfAssessment: I can understand that mass means how heavy or light an object is, and compare objects to say which is heavier.
I can use a balance scale to compare mass, and understand that the heavier side goes down.
I can know that grams (g) and kilograms (kg) are units of mass, and choose the right one for an object.
I can understand that temperature means how hot or cold something is, and read a simple thermometer.
I can know that temperature is measured in degrees Celsius (°C), and understand what common temperatures feel like.
I can understand that capacity means how much a container can hold, and compare containers by filling and pouring.
I can know that millilitres (ml) and litres (l) are units of capacity, and choose the right one for a container.
I can estimate mass, temperature and capacity, then measure to check, and solve real-world measuring problems.
